text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.

## How to do the work — completeness over polish

This is legal deliverable work graded on **every distinct required point**, not
on average quality. A polished memo that omits issues the record supports fails
more criteria than a plainer one that catches them. Work to the method below on
any matter.

**Read everything before you conclude.** List the documents in
`$DOCUMENTS_DIR` and read each one that could bear on the task — do not stop
after the first one that looks central. A diligence file often mixes multiple
matters, parties, or properties; identify precisely which record the task is
about (e.g. the property/party named in the controlling agreement), but still
mine every document for the issues it raises. Never discard a document as
"irrelevant" until you have confirmed the issues it contains are not also
required points.

**Enumerate issues exhaustively, one per item.** Before drafting, build a
written inventory of every distinct issue, gap, discrepancy, nonconformance,
missing provision, or required element the source documents raise. Treat each as
its own item — do not bundle several into one bullet. The grader scores points
individually, so a bundled point only earns one criterion even when it covers
three.

**Cross-check the deliverable against that inventory.** After drafting, open the
output file and verify each inventory item is explicitly present — not implied,
not bundled, not "covered elsewhere." Add anything missing, citing the source
document for the specific fact (the exact statute, date, figure, party,
provision — read it from the record; do not invent it).

**Cover every required dimension and every item in scope.** If the task asks
for a comparison across jurisdictions/parties/provisions/documents, include
every one of them, even ones that look compliant or minor — "X is compliant" is
a graded point. If the task names multiple output files, produce all of them.
If a source document lists N items (e.g. N tanks, N facilities, N criteria),
address each by name.

**Do not fabricate.** Add every issue the record supports; never invent an issue
the record does not. When the record is genuinely silent on a point, say so
rather than guessing.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.
Review the attached PERM application against the beneficiary's supporting credentials and produce a gap analysis memo with remedial recommendations. Output: `credential-gap-analysis.docx`.
I'll start by exploring the workspace to understand what documents are available for this PERM application review.

In her role as Software Engineer, Ms. Mehrotra\'s primary responsibilities centered on backend software development for our enterprise SaaS products. A significant portion of her work involved building and maintaining **data pipelines for ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes** that supported our client-facing analytics platform. Ms. Mehrotra participated in the full software development lifecycle, including requirements gathering, system design, coding, unit and integration testing, and production deployment. She worked extensively with relational databases, including MySQL and Oracle, and with enterprise middleware systems used to integrate disparate application components. Throughout her tenure, she collaborated closely with cross-functional teams, including product management and quality assurance, to deliver reliable and scalable software solutions on schedule.

**[Section 1: Scope of Evaluation]{.underline}**

This evaluation covers **only** the Bachelor of Technology (B.Tech) degree awarded by Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune (MIT-Pune), affiliated with Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune, India. This evaluation does **not** cover any other academic credentials held by the applicant. The applicant also holds a Master of Science (M.S.) in Computer Science from North Carolina State University, Raleigh, North Carolina, a regionally accredited U.S. institution; that degree does not require a foreign credential evaluation as it is a domestic credential. IAE was asked to evaluate the foreign undergraduate credential only.

**[Section 2: Documents Reviewed]{.underline}**

The following documents were reviewed in preparing this evaluation:

1\. **Official Transcript (Marksheets)** --- Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune (MIT-Pune), covering all eight semesters of the B.Tech program (academic years 2009--2013), issued by the Office of the Controller of Examinations, Savitribai Phule Pune University.

2\. **Degree Certificate / Convocation Certificate** --- Bachelor of Technology in Electronics and Communication Engineering, awarded June 2013, bearing the seal of Savitribai Phule Pune University.

3\. **Course-by-Course Syllabus Description** --- Obtained from MIT-Pune\'s Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering (2009--2013 catalog).

4\. **University Grants Commission (UGC) Recognition Verification** --- Confirming the status of Savitribai Phule Pune University as a recognized Indian university under the UGC Act, 1956.

**[Section 3: Institutional Background]{.underline}**

**Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune (MIT-Pune)** is a private engineering college located in Pune, Maharashtra, India, established in 1983. MIT-Pune is affiliated with **Savitribai Phule Pune University** (formerly University of Pune), a public state university recognized by the University Grants Commission (UGC) of India. Savitribai Phule Pune University is accredited by the National Assessment and Accreditation Council (NAAC) with an \"A+\" grade. The B.Tech program at MIT-Pune is approved by the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE). The program follows a four-year (eight-semester) curriculum as prescribed by the affiliating university. Admissions to the B.Tech program are competitive and based on national and state-level entrance examinations.

**[Section 4: Program Description and Curriculum Analysis]{.underline}**

The Bachelor of Technology in Electronics and Communication Engineering at MIT-Pune is a four-year, full-time undergraduate program comprising eight semesters. The program represents approximately **180 semester credit hours** when converted to U.S. credit-hour equivalency. The core curriculum includes mathematics (calculus, linear algebra, probability and statistics), physics, chemistry (first year), analog and digital electronics, communication systems, signal processing, electromagnetic theory, microprocessors, VLSI design, control systems, and engineering electives. The program includes a final-year capstone project and thesis.

The curriculum contains limited computer-related coursework --- specifically \"Introduction to Programming (C/C++),\" \"Data Structures,\" and \"Computer Organization\" --- constituting approximately 12--15 credit hours out of the approximately 180 total credit hours. The core emphasis of the program is on electronics and communication engineering.

Dr. Mehrotra graduated with **First Class honors** and a **CGPA of 8.2 on a 10-point scale** as prescribed by the university. Her graduation date was **June 2013**.

**[Section 5: Evaluation Findings and Equivalency Determination]{.underline}**

**Finding:** Based on IAE\'s analysis of the institutional accreditation, program structure, curriculum content, credit-hour equivalency, and grading standards, it is the professional opinion of International Academic Evaluators that Dr. Ananya Mehrotra\'s **Bachelor of Technology (B.Tech) in Electronics and Communication Engineering** from Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune, affiliated with Savitribai Phule Pune University, India, awarded in **June 2013**, is equivalent to a **bachelor\'s degree in Electronics and Communication Engineering from a regionally accredited institution in the United States**.

The degree represents the completion of a four-year undergraduate course of study equivalent to approximately 180 U.S. semester credit hours.

The CGPA of 8.2/10 translates to an approximate U.S. GPA of **3.3 on a 4.0 scale**, indicating above-average academic performance.

**[Section 6: Methodology Statement]{.underline}**

IAE evaluates foreign academic credentials in accordance with standards established by NACES and AICE. The evaluation considers: (1) the accreditation and recognition status of the issuing institution; (2) the admission requirements of the program; (3) the duration and structure of the program; (4) the curriculum and credit-hour equivalency; and (5) the grading system and academic standing. IAE\'s evaluators hold advanced degrees in international education and have expertise in the educational systems of over 100 countries. This evaluation is based on documentation provided and institutional information available as of the report date.

**[Section 7: Limitations and Disclaimers]{.underline}**

This evaluation represents the professional opinion of International Academic Evaluators and is intended for use in U.S. immigration proceedings. IAE does not guarantee acceptance of this evaluation by any government agency, employer, or educational institution. This evaluation is limited to the credential specifically identified in the Scope of Evaluation (Section 1) and does not extend to any other academic or professional credentials held by the applicant. The evaluation is based on documents and information available as of **February 15, 2025**. Any subsequent changes to the applicant\'s academic record are not reflected herein. This report should not be construed as an evaluation of the applicant\'s professional competence or fitness for any particular position.

\[IAE Official Seal\]

INTERNATIONAL ACADEMIC EVALUATORS

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Date: February 15, 2025

For verification of this report, please contact International Academic Evaluators at evaluations@iae-credentials.org or (212) 555-0847.

result:
"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

{"file_path": "memo.md", "content": "# MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION**

| | |
|---|---|
| **TO:** | Melanie Foss, HR Director, Prismex Analytics, Inc. (cc: Rajiv Sunderajan, Chief Executive Officer) |
| **FROM:** | Craig Halloran, Esq., Whitmore & Callahan LLP |
| **DATE:** | June 9, 2025 |
| **RE:** | Credential Gap Analysis — Certified PERM Application (ETA Form 9089), Case No. A-18247-63910 — Beneficiary: Ananya Mehrotra — Position: Senior Machine Learning Engineer |

---

## I. Introduction and Scope

This memorandum responds to your June 4, 2025 transmittal of the petition package for Dr. Ananya Mehrotra and your request that we (1) identify any missing or supplemental documents, (2) flag any issues with the credentials matching the PERM requirements, and (3) advise on next steps. Pursuant to that request, we have reviewed the certified PERM application (ETA Form 9089, Case No. A-18247-63910, certified May 22, 2025) against each of the beneficiary's supporting credentials and identified the gaps, discrepancies, and nonconformances set forth below, together with remedial recommendations for each.

**Scope note.** This memorandum is limited to a gap analysis of the certified PERM application against the beneficiary's supporting credentials. The H-1B (I-129) cap-gap, status-continuation, and filing-timeline questions raised in your email are addressed under separate cover. We note for clarity that a certified PERM application underlies the **Form I-140 immigrant petition** (employment-based permanent residence); the references in your email to "moving forward with the I-129" appear to conflate the nonimmigrant H-1B process with the immigrant process that the certified PERM supports. We will confirm the overall strategy with you separately.

**Documents reviewed.** (1) Certified PERM application, ETA Form 9089, Case No. A-18247-63910; (2) beneficiary résumé/CV; (3) experience letter from DataBridge Solutions LLC (Dr. Samuel Okonkwo, VP of Data Science, dated April 10, 2025); (4) experience letter from Evalpoint Technologies Pvt. Ltd. (Vikram Reddy, Director of Engineering, dated March 28, 2025); (5) NC State University official transcript (M.S. in Computer Science, issued May 20, 2025); (6) IAE credential evaluation report (IAE-2025-02-4738, dated February 15, 2025); (7) compiled professional certifications (Whitmore & Callahan LLP, June 2025); and (8) Prevailing Wage Determination (PWD Tracking No. P-200-24187-329156, dated November 8, 2024).

---

## II. Executive Summary

The beneficiary's **education** (M.S. in Computer Science, NC State, May 2018) and **core post-master's experience** at DataBridge Solutions LLC satisfy the PERM's primary education and 5-year progressive post-master's experience minimums, and the **offered wage ($142,000) exceeds the prevailing wage ($131,747)** with the PWD within its validity period. Those foundational elements are sound.

However, our review identified **one critical gap, several material discrepancies, and a number of documentation and consistency items** that must be resolved before the I-140 is filed and to place the certified PERM on defensible footing in the event of an audit. The most serious finding is that the PERM's Section J.B.5 special requirement of the **AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification (or equivalent)** is **not held by the beneficiary**, and the two certifications she does hold do not satisfy that requirement — yet Section K.3 of the certified PERM attests that she possesses all special skills and requirements. This creates audit, revocation, and I-140 exposure and is the central issue addressed below.

A prioritized summary of all findings appears in the table in Section III. Detailed analysis and remedial recommendations follow in Sections IV–V.

---

## III. Summary of Findings

| # | Issue | Source of Requirement / Record | Severity | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Required AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification is **not held** by the beneficiary | PERM § J.B.5; Certifications Compilation, Verification Notes ¶ 3 | **Critical** | Gap |
| 2 | Neither held certification is an "equivalent"; AWS Cloud Practitioner is **foundational and expired** (exp. 1/18/2025, before filing); TensorFlow Developer Certificate is Google-issued and framework-specific, not AWS ML–equivalent | PERM § J.B.5; Certifications Compilation, Certs. 1–2 | **Critical** | Gap |
| 3 | PERM § K.3 attests beneficiary possesses all J.B.5 special requirements (incl. the AWS ML Specialty cert) — inconsistent with the credential record | PERM § K.3 vs. Certifications Compilation | **Critical** | Attestation discrepancy |
| 4 | Certification requirement may not be "normal to the occupation" / may require business-necessity justification under 20 CFR 656.17(h) | PERM § J.B.5; 20 CFR 656.17 | **High** | Audit-vulnerability |
| 5 | "Proficiency in Python and R" (J.B.5(e)) — R is documented only as "intermediate"/"some use," not proficiency | PERM § J.B.5(e); Résumé; DataBridge letter | **Material** | Gap |
| 6 | **FEIN mismatch**: PERM § C.7 lists 84-3291756; PWD § 1 lists 84-3291047 | PERM § C.7; PWD § 1 | **Material** | Discrepancy |
| 7 | **Missing experience verification letter** for NC State Research Assistant position (Aug 2016–May 2018); Prof. Tsai on sabbatical | PERM § J.C, Entry 2; HR email; NC State transcript | **Moderate** | Documentation gap |
| 8 | **Missing underlying foreign-degree evidence** (B.Tech degree certificate and marksheets) — only the IAE evaluation is in the package | IAE Report § 2; HR email inventory item 6 | **Moderate** | Documentation gap |
| 9 | "Dr." title used throughout, but highest documented degree is an M.S.; no doctorate in the record | PERM § I.1; Résumé; experience letters; IAE Report | **Moderate** | Consistency/accuracy |
| 10 | "Approximately 7 years" post-master's experience (K.2) is ~6 yrs 9 mos as of the 3/15/2025 filing (still > 5-yr minimum) | PERM § K.2 | **Low** | Precision |
| 11 | Post-master's 5-yr requirement met **only** by DataBridge; Evalpoint (pre-master's, software eng.) and RA (pre-master's, part-time) do not count toward it | PERM § J.C, Entries 1–3; experience letters | **Low** | Characterization |
| 12 | Supervisory scope: position requires supervising 3–5 ML engineers; beneficiary led 2 junior data scientists | PERM § J.B.5 / H.10; DataBridge letter | **Low** | Observation (likely a job condition) |
| 13 | HR Director phone: PERM/PWD (512) 555-0174 vs. email signature (512) 555-0184 | PERM § C.11; PWD § 1; HR email | **Low** | Consistency |
| 14 | Experience letters post-date the PERM filing (3/15/2025) — acceptable, but note timing | DataBridge letter (4/10/2025); Evalpoint letter (3/28/2025) | **Informational** | Acceptable |
| 15 | Résumé "7+ years of experience" (total, incl. pre-master's) vs. PERM "7 years post-master's" (DataBridge only) — avoid conflation | Résumé; PERM § K.2 | **Informational** | Clarity |

**Confirmed compliant/consistent items** (addressed in Section V): M.S. satisfies the education minimum; IAE evaluation properly scoped to the foreign B.Tech; offered wage exceeds prevailing wage; PWD within validity; SOC 15-2051 consistent across PERM and PWD; PWD minimums consistent with PERM; special skills (a)–(d) supported by DataBridge experience; progressive experience shown by promotion; no qualifying experience gained with the petitioning employer.

---

## IV. Detailed Gap Analysis and Remedial Recommendations

### A. Certification Requirement (Critical) — Issues 1–4

**1. The required AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification is not held by the beneficiary.**

PERM Section J.B.5 states that the position requires the "AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent." Section K.3 of the certified PERM attests that the beneficiary "possess[es] the special skills and other requirements stated in Section J.B.5." The compiled certifications, however, contain only two credentials, and the Certification Verification Notes expressly state that "counsel specifically inquired whether the beneficiary holds the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or any other AWS specialty-level certification; the beneficiary confirmed she does not hold any such certification." The beneficiary therefore does not meet this minimum special requirement as attested.

**2. Neither held certification satisfies the "or equivalent" language.**

- **AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner** (Validation No. AWS-CCP-2022-0418-MEHROTRA): This is a **foundational-level** credential that, per the compilation, "does not demonstrate specialization in any specific AWS service domain, including machine learning." It is **not** equivalent to the AWS ML – Specialty certification. Moreover, it **expired on January 18, 2025** — approximately two months **before** the PERM filing date of March 15, 2025 — and the beneficiary has not renewed it. It was therefore expired at the time of filing and at certification.
- **TensorFlow Developer Certificate** (Validation No. TF-DEV-2023-0310-AM, issued March 10, 2023, no expiration): This is issued by **Google**, is **framework-specific to TensorFlow**, and "is not affiliated with Amazon Web Services (AWS)." It validates TensorFlow model-building skill, not AWS-based ML deployment or the breadth of the AWS ML – Specialty scope. It is not an "equivalent."

Accordingly, the "or equivalent" alternative is not satisfied by either credential.

**3. The K.3 attestation is inconsistent with the credential record.**

Because J.B.5 includes the AWS ML – Specialty certification as a required special skill and K.3 attests that the beneficiary possesses all such special skills, the K.3 attestation is inconsistent with the documented credentials. Under 20 CFR Part 656, material misstatements in a PERM application can subject a certification to revocation and the employer (and beneficiary) to further consequences; the alien declaration on the form itself references potential penalties under 18 U.S.C. § 1546. This is the most significant exposure in the file.

**4. The certification requirement may not be "normal to the occupation."**

Under 20 CFR 656.17(h), a requirement that is not normal to the occupation must be justified by business necessity (i.e., job-relatedness and consistency with business necessity). A specific vendor specialty certification is arguably not normal to the Data Scientist occupation (SOC 15-2051). If the application is audited, DOL may challenge whether the certification requirement is normal and business-necessary — a vulnerability that is compounded by the fact that the beneficiary does not hold the credential.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 1):**

1. **Counsel the employer on the attestation discrepancy.** We will advise Prismex in writing of the inconsistency between K.3 and the credential record and the resulting audit, revocation, and I-140 exposure, and document the employer's informed decision on how to proceed.
2. **Obtain the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification for the beneficiary as quickly as practicable.** *Caution on timing:* for PERM purposes the beneficiary must have met the minimum requirements **at the time of filing** (March 15, 2025), and USCIS evaluates I-140 qualification as of the PERM filing date. A certification obtained after filing does not retroactively cure the K.3 attestation as of the filing date and may not cure the I-140 qualification analysis. We will assess whether the certification can be earned before I-140 filing/adjudication and how best to present it.
3. **Assemble business-necessity documentation** for the certification requirement (job-relatedness to the healthcare/fintech ML-deployment duties and business necessity), in anticipation of audit.
4. **Review the recruitment record** to confirm whether the certification was actually applied as a screening criterion against U.S. applicants. The recruitment report states the three applicants were rejected for lacking education and experience — not for lacking the certification. If the certification was not enforced against applicants, a "requirements not as stated" exposure exists and should be evaluated.
5. **Evaluate post-certification correction options.** DOL permits only limited amendments after certification; a substantive change to the minimum requirements generally cannot be made post-certification and would require refiling (new PWD and recruitment), which is in tension with the September 15, 2025 STEM OPT deadline. We will weigh refiling, proceeding with documented audit risk, and obtaining the certification, and recommend a path.
6. **Do not file the I-140** until the certification issue is resolved or a documented, informed risk decision is made by the employer.

### B. R Programming Proficiency (Material) — Issue 5

PERM Section J.B.5(e) and the job description (H.10) require "proficiency in Python and R programming languages." The record documents **expert** Python proficiency but characterizes R only as **"intermediate"** (résumé) and **"some use of R for statistical reporting and ad hoc data analysis"** (DataBridge letter). "Some use"/"intermediate" may not rise to "proficiency" and could be challenged as insufficient to meet the attested minimum.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 2):**

1. Obtain **supplemental evidence of R proficiency** — e.g., an amended or supplemental experience letter from DataBridge detailing specific R-based deliverables, packages, and models; R-based project artifacts; or R training/certification.
2. If the beneficiary's R skills are genuinely proficient, document them with specifics (statistical reporting products, R packages used, models built in R).
3. If R proficiency cannot be substantiated at the "proficiency" level, assess whether "intermediate"/"some use" is defensible (likely insufficient) and whether the requirement should be revisited for audit-defense purposes. Note that R is more normal to the Data Scientist occupation than the AWS certification, so the requirement itself is more defensible; the issue is the level of supporting evidence.

### C. FEIN Discrepancy (Material) — Issue 6

The PERM (Section C.7) lists the employer's Federal Employer Identification Number as **84-3291756**, while the Prevailing Wage Determination (Section 1) lists **84-3291047**. The two FEINs differ. Because the PWD was issued (November 8, 2024) before the PERM filing (March 15, 2025), and the PERM is certified under FEIN 84-3291756, this discrepancy must be reconciled before the I-140 is filed, as USCIS will verify the petitioner's identity against the certified PERM.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 2):**

1. Obtain the employer's **IRS FEIN confirmation** (e.g., CP 575 notice or a recent tax filing/payroll record from Cornerpoint Payroll Services) to establish the correct FEIN.
2. **Reconcile** the PERM and PWD FEINs. If the PERM FEIN (84-3291756) is correct, document the PWD discrepancy (the PWD is employer-input-based; the certified PERM FEIN governs for the I-140). If the PERM FEIN is erroneous, assess correction/explanation options for the I-140 and the audit exposure.
3. Ensure the **I-140 petitioner FEIN matches the certified PERM**; prepare an explanation of the PWD discrepancy for the record.

### D. Missing NC State Research Assistant Experience Letter (Moderate) — Issue 7

As you flagged in your email, the package does not include an experience verification letter from NC State University for the beneficiary's Research Assistant position (August 15, 2016 – May 15, 2018). Professor Helen Tsai (the RA supervisor and thesis advisor) is on sabbatical and has not responded. The official transcript does document the appointment — the "Graduate Assistantship Appointments" section records a Research Assistant appointment, August 15, 2016 – May 15, 2018, supervised by Professor Tsai, funded under NSF Grant No. IIS-1742956 — but there is no separate employer experience letter.

**Mitigating context.** This RA experience is **pre-master's** (it predates the May 12, 2018 M.S. conferral) and **part-time (20 hours/week)**. It therefore does **not** count toward the 5-year *post-master's* experience requirement, which is satisfied solely by the DataBridge experience. The gap is accordingly lower-risk, but it should still be closed for audit completeness because the position is listed as an experience entry on the PERM (Section J.C, Entry 2).

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 3):**

1. Pursue an experience verification letter from NC State through alternative channels: (a) the Department of Computer Science administrator or NC State HR; or (b) a thesis committee member — Professor Mark R. Linden or Professor Sonia K. Patel — who can corroborate the RA appointment, dates, and duties.
2. In the interim, rely on the **official transcript's Graduate Assistantship Appointments record** (plus the thesis record) as partial documentation of the appointment.
3. If the RA experience is not needed to meet any minimum requirement, consider whether it should remain listed on the PERM experience record or be de-emphasized in the petition narrative to avoid inviting scrutiny of part-time, pre-master's experience.

### E. Missing Underlying Foreign-Degree Evidence (Moderate) — Issue 8

The petition package inventory lists the IAE credential evaluation report for the B.Tech, but does not list the **underlying foreign-degree documents** (the B.Tech degree/convocation certificate and the eight-semester marksheets/transcripts). The IAE report (Section 2) states that it reviewed these documents, but USCIS requires the **actual foreign degree evidence** in addition to the evaluation. Relying on the evaluation alone is insufficient.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 3):**

1. Include **copies of the B.Tech degree certificate (convocation certificate) and the official marksheets/transcripts** (all eight semesters, as issued by Savitribai Phule Pune University / MIT-Pune) in the petition package.
2. Optionally include the **NC State M.S. diploma** for completeness (the transcript, which shows conferral on May 12, 2018, is sufficient standing alone, but the diploma may be added).

### F. "Dr." Title / Credential Accuracy (Moderate) — Issue 9

The beneficiary is referred to as **"Dr. Ananya Mehrotra"** throughout the record — including the PERM (Section I.1: "also known as: Dr. Ananya Mehrotra"), the résumé, both experience letters, the IAE evaluation, and the certifications compilation. Her **highest documented degree, however, is the M.S. in Computer Science** (NC State, 2018); the record reflects no doctoral degree (no Ph.D. appears in the transcript, the IAE evaluation, or the résumé). The "Dr." title is therefore unsupported by the documented credentials and could be read to imply a doctorate that the beneficiary does not hold.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 3):**

1. **Confirm** with the beneficiary whether she holds any doctoral degree not reflected in the package. If she does, document it.
2. If no doctorate is held, **correct the title** to "Ms." or use "Ananya Mehrotra" without "Dr." in the I-140/I-129 filings and supporting documents, and ensure consistency across the package, to avoid any implication of a doctoral credential.
3. Note that the certified PERM already uses "also known as: Dr. Ananya Mehrotra" (Section I.1); this is harmless in the certified application but should be reconciled going forward.

### G. Experience Calculation and Characterization (Low) — Issues 10–11

**Issue 10 — "Approximately 7 years" precision.** Section K.2 states the beneficiary has "approximately 7 years of post-master's experience at DataBridge Solutions LLC (June 2018 – present)." Measured precisely, the DataBridge tenure is approximately **6 years and 9 months** as of the March 15, 2025 filing date, and approximately **6 years and 11.5 months** as of the May 22, 2025 certification date. Both figures exceed the 5-year minimum, but "approximately 7 years" slightly overstates the figure as of filing. We recommend using precise figures in the petition narrative.

**Issue 11 — Source of the post-master's experience.** The 5-year progressive *post-master's* requirement is met **solely** by the DataBridge experience (June 4, 2018 – present). The Evalpoint experience (July 2013 – July 2016) is **pre-master's** and is, per the Evalpoint letter, primarily backend software engineering in which ML exposure was "ancillary and secondary." The NC State RA experience (August 2016 – May 2018) is **pre-master's and part-time (20 hrs/week)**. Neither counts toward the post-master's requirement. The petition narrative should make clear that the qualifying post-master's experience is the DataBridge tenure only, and should not conflate the pre-master's roles with the post-master's qualifying experience.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 4):** Use precise post-master's figures; clearly attribute the 5-year qualifying experience to DataBridge alone; avoid relying on (or implying reliance on) the pre-master's, part-time, or non-ML roles to satisfy the post-master's minimum.

### H. Supervisory Scope (Low / Observation) — Issue 12

The position requires supervising "a team of 3–5 ML engineers" (J.B.5; H.10). The beneficiary's documented supervisory experience is leading "a team of 2 junior data scientists" at DataBridge. The supervision of 3–5 engineers is phrased in J.B.5 as a forward-looking job condition ("Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers") rather than as a prior-experience prerequisite ("Experience must include…"), so it is most likely **not** a credential gap. We flag it only so that counsel can confirm the employer's intent.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 4):** Confirm that supervision of 3–5 engineers is a job condition (not a prior-experience requirement). If so, no further action is required beyond ensuring the I-140 job description matches the certified PERM. If the employer intended prior supervisory experience over 3–5 as a requirement, note that the documented scope (2) is below 3–5 and assess audit exposure.

### I. Minor Consistency and Informational Items (Low) — Issues 13–15

**Issue 13 — HR Director telephone number.** The PERM (Section C.11) and the PWD (Section 1) list the HR Director's phone as (512) 555-0174, while your email signature lists (512) 555-0184. Confirm the correct number and ensure consistency in the filings.

**Issue 14 — Experience-letter timing.** The DataBridge letter (April 10, 2025) and the Evalpoint letter (March 28, 2025) both post-date the PERM filing (March 15, 2025). This is generally acceptable for PERM audit purposes — the letters document experience gained before filing, and both clearly state pre-filing dates of employment — but we note the timing for the record.

**Issue 15 — Résumé "7+ years" phrasing.** The résumé's professional summary states "7+ years of experience spanning software development, applied research, and production data science," which measures **total** experience across all four roles (including pre-master's roles). This is a different measure from the PERM's "approximately 7 years of *post-master's*" experience (DataBridge only). Ensure the petition narrative does not conflate the two.

**Remedial recommendations (Priority 4):** Reconcile the phone number; note the letter timing (acceptable); and distinguish the résumé's total-experience figure from the PERM's post-master's figure in the petition narrative.

---

## V. Confirmed Compliant and Consistent Items

For completeness, the following elements of the certified PERM are supported by, and consistent with, the supporting credentials, and require no remedial action:

1. **Education minimum (J.B.1).** The M.S. in Computer Science from NC State (conferred May 12, 2018) satisfies the "Master's degree in Computer Science, Machine Learning, or a closely related field" requirement. (PERM § J.B.1; NC State transcript.)
2. **Foreign credential evaluation.** The IAE evaluation (NACES-member, Report No. IAE-2025-02-4738, February 15, 2025) is properly scoped to the foreign B.Tech only and finds it equivalent to a U.S. bachelor's in Electronics and Communication Engineering; the domestic M.S. requires no foreign evaluation. (IAE Report §§ 1, 5.)
3. **Offered wage vs. prevailing wage.** The offered wage of $142,000/year exceeds the prevailing wage of $131,747/year (Level III). (PERM §§ E.5, H.6, N.2; PWD § 3.)
4. **PWD validity.** The PWD (issued November 8, 2024; valid through November 7, 2025) was valid at the time of the March 15, 2025 filing. (PWD; PERM § E.)
5. **SOC code consistency.** SOC 15-2051 (Data Scientists) is consistent across the PERM and the PWD. (PERM §§ E.2, H.3; PWD § 2.)
6. **PWD minimums consistent with PERM.** The PWD's minimum education (Master's) and minimum experience (5 years) are consistent with PERM §§ J.B.1 and J.B.3. (PWD § 2; PERM § J.B.)
7. **Special skills (a)–(d) supported.** The DataBridge Data Scientist II experience supports (a) designing/deploying deep learning models with TensorFlow and PyTorch; (b) Apache Spark (MLlib) distributed training; (c) NLP pipeline development (NER, sentiment analysis); and (d) AWS SageMaker deployment. (PERM § J.B.5(a)–(d); DataBridge letter § 3.)
8. **Progressive experience.** The promotion from Data Scientist I to Data Scientist II (effective January 4, 2021) supports the "progressive" characterization of the post-master's experience. (PERM § J.B.3; DataBridge letter §§ 1–3.)
9. **No qualifying experience with the petitioning employer.** The beneficiary has never been employed by Prismex Analytics (PERM §§ I.10, I.11, K.5), so no experience gained with the petitioning employer is being used to qualify — no issue under the restrictions on using petitioning-employer experience.

---

## VI. Prioritized Action Plan

**Priority 1 — Critical (resolve before I-140 filing):**
- Reconcile the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification gap (Issues 1–3): counsel the employer on the K.3 attestation discrepancy; pursue the certification for the beneficiary; assemble business-necessity documentation; review the recruitment record for enforcement of the requirement; and evaluate correction/refiling options against the September 15, 2025 STEM OPT deadline.

**Priority 2 — Material:**
- Document R proficiency or assess the requirement (Issue 5).
- Reconcile the FEIN mismatch between the PERM and the PWD (Issue 6).

**Priority 3 — Moderate (documentation/accuracy):**
- Obtain an NC State RA experience verification letter via alternative channels (Issue 7).
- Add the underlying B.Tech degree certificate and marksheets to the package (Issue 8).
- Reconcile the "Dr." title with the documented (master's-level) credentials (Issue 9).

**Priority 4 — Low / consistency:**
- Use precise post-master's experience figures and attribute qualifying experience to DataBridge only (Issues 10–11).
- Confirm the supervisory-scope item is a job condition (Issue 12).
- Reconcile the HR phone number; note letter timing; distinguish résumé total-experience from PERM post-master's experience (Issues 13–15).

---

## VII. Risk Assessment and Next Steps

The certified PERM is, on its face, approvable as to education, core experience, and wage. The **decisive risk** is the certification gap in Section J.B.5, compounded by the K.3 attestation: the beneficiary does not hold the required AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification (or an equivalent), yet the certified application attests that she does. This exposure is live for (a) a DOL audit/revocation of the certification and (b) USCIS adjudication of the I-140, where the beneficiary's qualifications are evaluated as of the PERM filing date. The secondary risks are the R-proficiency evidence, the FEIN discrepancy, and the documentation gaps (RA letter; underlying foreign-degree evidence).

We recommend that Prismex **not authorize I-140 filing** until the Priority 1 certification issue is resolved or a documented, informed risk decision is recorded. Given the September 15, 2025 STEM OPT expiration, we will move quickly to (i) confirm the certification path and timing, (ii) assemble business-necessity and recruitment-consistency documentation, and (iii) reconcile the FEIN and documentation items in parallel, and we will report back with a recommended filing strategy and timeline.

Please let us know if you would like to schedule a call with Rajiv to review the Priority 1 items. We are available at your convenience.

*This memorandum is privileged and confidential, constitutes attorney work product, and is intended solely for the named recipients in connection with the above-referenced matter.*"}
